addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**docs: apply max review #1 (0.62.0)**

Apply accepted items from
[max-review-1.md](design-cli/max-review-1.md) to the
por/dual parity audit doc and the copying stub.
Concern #1 + #2 Topology were applied in an
abandoned-during-rebase snapshot in 0.61.0
(`604fb9e8`); the rest of the review (concerns #2
Privacy, #3–#6, nits N1–N4, process observation)
remains open.

- 0.62.0-0 Preparation (current)
- 0.62.0-1 replay abandoned audit-doc edits (Concern
  #1 three spots + Concern #2 Topology)
- 0.62.0-N walk remaining items (concerns then nits);
  each accepted item lands as its own commit
- 0.62.0 close-out

## Ideas

 Items not yet solid enough for `## Todo` (or surfaced
 during close-out / end-of-day before they're fully
 formed). Triaged at the next Preparation: promote to
 `## Todo` / `notes/todo-backlog.md`, fold into a
 picked-up cycle, or drop.

1. **Codify ochid invariant + bot-repo rules + squash
   gating + cross-repo migration in
   `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.** Was planned for
   `0.59.0-2` but deferred when 0.59.0 closed out via
   squash + Option F (manual bot-side rewrite). The
   rules were exercised manually for the close-out;
   this Idea formally codifies them.
   - Codify the **ochid invariant** in `## ochid
     trailers`: every public ochid must resolve in the
     public graph.
   - Codify bot-repo rules: never squashed; descriptions
     editable (`jj describe` preserves chid).
   - Codify squash gating: until `vc-x1 push --squash`
     exists, manual symmetric squash (Option F: app
     squash + bot-side trailer rewrite + force-push) is

100	
101	1. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**
102	   Today push assumes 1:1 symmetric WC commits with shared
103	   title/body. The new cycle protocol has a different shape on
104	   each side:
105	   - code side: fully committed before push (N commits via
106	     merge), nothing to commit at push time
107	   - `.claude` side: one commit with its own message (distinct
108	     from any code commit) and a multi-line `ochid:` listing
109	     all N code commits
110	
111	   Teach push to:
112	   - detect this shape
113	   - skip `commit-app` when code WC is empty
114	   - compose a `.claude`-specific message
115	   - emit multi-line `ochid:` per the design in [[10]]
116	
117	   Today's workaround: pre-commit `.claude` manually, then
118	   `vc-x1 push <bm> --from bookmark-both --yes`.
119	2. **vc-x1 push --squash: symmetric squash on both repos.**
120	   Automate Option F (manually exercised in 0.59.0
121	   close-out): app-side squash + bot-side description
122	   rewrite + force-push, atomically. Without this,
123	   squash is a manual recipe that future cycles must
124	   follow each time.
125	   - App side: squash cycle commits into one new commit;
126	     capture the squashed chid.
127	   - Bot side: rewrite the prior push commit's
128	     description — replace its per-commit `ochid:`
129	     trailers with one pointing at the squashed chid;
130	     add a rewrite-note acknowledging the change
131	     (preserves historical truth for future readers).
132	   - Force-push bot `main` (rewrites the published
133	     commit; chid preserved via `jj describe`).
134	   - Push app `main`. The new bot commit paired with
135	     this push receives `ochid: /<squashed-chid>` as
136	     normal — produces a 2:1 bot→code pattern that's
137	     already in the protocol's design space.
138	   - Gates `Squash to one commit` as a routine
139	     close-out shape vs. the current manual recipe.
140	3. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
141	   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
142	   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
143	   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
144	   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
145	   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
146	   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
147	   (`init.rs`, tests).
148

file.content:
squash + bot-side trailer rewrite + force-push) is
     the standard recipe; merge non-ff is the default
     shape for multi-commit cycles.
   - Sketch cross-repo migration: ochids change at
     every merge until the change reaches the canonical
     repo's `main`.
   - Apply Ideas-aware Preparation/Close-out updates:
     Preparation triages `## Ideas` (promote / fold /
     drop) before declaring the plan; Close-out
     captures unresolved follow-ups into `## Ideas`.

## Priorities

- P1 is highest priority, same priority are grouped equally
- A line with `...` is a prefix-match against a `## Todo` title
  - resolves locally (in this file); should be unique
- Entries without a prefix-match are free-form
  - chores tasks, ad-hoc reminders.

### P1

- `**vc-x1 push: support...**`
- `**vc-x1 push --squash...**`

### P2

- `**single-field...**`

### P3

- `**por -> dual...**`

## Todo

 Prioritized entries (referenced by `## Priorities`) lower priority
 todo's are in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). In all cases
 we use the [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form). When
 more detail is waranted those reside in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`.
 Also, we use the  design subsections (link via `[N]` ref). Run
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.

1. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**
   Today push assumes 1:1 symmetric WC commits with shared
   title/body. The new cycle protocol has a different shape on
   each side:
   - code side: fully committed before push (N commits via
     merge), nothing to commit at push time
   - `.claude` side: one commit with its own message (distinct
     from any code commit) and a multi-line `ochid:` listing
     all N code commits

   Teach push to:
   - detect this shape
   - skip `commit-app` when code WC is empty
   - compose a `.claude`-specific message
   - emit multi-line `ochid:` per the design in [[10]]

   Today's workaround: pre-commit `.claude` manually, then
   `vc-x1 push <bm> --from bookmark-both --yes`.
2. **vc-x1 push --squash: symmetric squash on both repos.**
   Automate Option F (manually exercised in 0.59.0
   close-out): app-side squash + bot-side description
   rewrite + force-push, atomically. Without this,
   squash is a manual recipe that future cycles must
   follow each time.
   - App side: squash cycle commits into one new commit;
     capture the squashed chid.
   - Bot side: rewrite the prior push commit's
     description — replace its per-commit `ochid:`
     trailers with one pointing at the squashed chid;
     add a rewrite-note acknowledging the change
     (preserves historical truth for future readers).
   - Force-push bot `main` (rewrites the published
     commit; chid preserved via `jj describe`).
   - Push app `main`. The new bot commit paired with
     this push receives `ochid: /<squashed-chid>` as
     normal — produces a 2:1 bot→code pattern that's
     already in the protocol's design space.
   - Gates `Squash to one commit` as a routine
     close-out shape vs. the current manual recipe.
3. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
   (`init.rs`, tests).

   Note: can a single field be defined as an type or enum instead

content.content:
## docs: apply max review #1 (0.62.0)

Commits: TBD (backfills at next cycle's Preparation)

Apply accepted items from
[`max-review-1.md`](../design-cli/max-review-1.md) to
the por/dual parity audit doc. The review file is the
cycle's working list: `0.62.0-1` seeds the full
capture, each later `-N` applies one item and deletes
it from the list (git history holds the original).

- **Concern #1 — runtime `--por` semantics** (0.62.0-2).
  Three doc-text spots (Topology Decisions "Runtime
  override" bullet, "Por's view of the chain" bullet,
  matrix Topology-column sub-bullet) read runtime
  `--por` as code-privileged (`→ Scope([Code])`, "just
  my code"). Rewrote all three to the target-relative
  rule: `--por` declares the target repo (`.` or
  `-R/--repo <path>`) single and suppresses sibling
  discovery symmetrically across code and bot — it
  doesn't privilege a side.
- **`--mode=<single|dual>` topology surface** (0.62.0-3).
  Concern #2 flagged the contradictory "exactly-one-of
  `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" phrasing. Rather
  than reword, replaced the two boolean flags with one
  value-bearing flag `--mode=<single|dual>` (default
  `dual`, `single` ≡ por, optional `s` / `d` aliases) —
  a single flag can't conflict with itself, so the
  contradiction dissolves. Propagated across the design
  sections (Topology Decisions, Por's view, matrix +
  quick-reference, Mapping section, Gap #8); the runtime
  override from Concern #1 becomes `--mode=single`. Audit
  findings (`## 1`–`## 8`) keep `--por` — they describe
  today's code.
- **`--visibility` + clone-row fix** (0.62.0-4). Resolves
  Concern #2's Privacy half the same way `--mode` did for
  Topology: Privacy axis adopts
  `--visibility=<public|private>` (default `public`,
  `--private` / `--public` kept as shortcuts),
  **`init`-only**. Empirically confirmed (`init -h`,
  `clone -h`, `clone.rs`) that `clone` never provisions a
  remote — so the matrix's clone row is corrected from
  `A/R ✓ Priv ✓` to `—` for both, with a footnote. Closes
  Concern #2.

Apply accepted items from
[`max-review-1.md`](../design-cli/max-review-1.md) to
the por/dual parity audit doc. The review file is the
cycle's working list: `0.62.0-1` seeds the full
capture, each later `-N` applies one item and deletes

  my code"). Rewrote all three to the target-relative

  discovery symmetrically across code and bot — it
  doesn't privilege a side.

  Concern #2 flagged the contradictory "exactly-one-of

  than reword, replaced the two boolean flags with one
  value-bearing flag `--mode=<single|dual>` (default
  `dual`, `single` ≡ por, optional `s` / `d` aliases) —
  a single flag can't conflict with itself, so the
  contradiction dissolves. Propagated across the design
  sections (Topology Decisions, Por's view, matrix +
  quick-reference, Mapping section, Gap #8); the runtime
  override from Concern #1 becomes `--mode=single`. Audit

1	# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design
2	
3	Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
4	of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
5	review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
6	chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
7	open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
8	disposition), or applied (already changed).
9	
10	## Scope reviewed
11	
12	- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
13	  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
14	- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
15	  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
16	- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
17	  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
18	  critiqued).
19	- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
20	  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
21	  cycle narrative.
22	
23	Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
24	close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
25	durable references.
26	
27	## Strengths
28	
29	For balance — what the bot thinks works well:
30	
31	- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
32	  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
33	  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
34	  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
35	  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
36	- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
37	  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
38	  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
39	  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
40	  a real defect of the bundle framing.
41	- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
42	  initially listed as independent; the design pass
43	  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
44	  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
45	- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
46	  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
47	  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
48	  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
49	  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
50	  elegantly.
51	- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
52	  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
53	  concrete equalization that validates the
54	  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
55	- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
56	  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
57	  itself for in 0.65.0.
58	
59	## Concerns
60	
61	### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability
62	
63	**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
64	`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
65	and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
66	property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
67	is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
68	src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
69	bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
70	the right thing.
71	
72	**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
73	`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
74	resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
75	Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
76	already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
77	muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
78	controls.
79	
80	**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
81	that cycle starts.
82	
83	### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out
84	
85	**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
86	(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
87	category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
88	isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
89	workspace with no remote for either side." The
90	bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
91	The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
92	first-class or rejected at parse time.
93	
94	**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
95	axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
96	interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
97	future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
98	confused error later in the workflow.
99	
100	**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
101	none` lands (Gap #5).
102	
103	### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout
104	
105	**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
106	any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
107	ignored — match the broader chain rule."
108	
109	The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
110	"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
111	axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
112	/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
113	choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
114	but it's not the obvious choice.
115	
116	**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
117	an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
118	list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
119	merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
120	same conversation in 0.62.0.
121	
122	**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.
123	
124	### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency
125	
126	**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
127	radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
128	detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
129	mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
130	validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
131	means broken-dual detection has to exist first.
132	
133	**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
134	"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
135	#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
136	renumbering.
137	
138	**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.
139	
140	## Nits
141	
142	Lower-priority items; do as time allows.
143	
144	- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
145	  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
146	  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
147	  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
148	  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
149	  hardening a use case nobody asked for.
150	
151	- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
152	  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
153	  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
154	  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
155	  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.
156	
157	- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
158	  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
159	  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
160	  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
161	  get consulted?") — a different angle.
162	
163	- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
164	  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
165	  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
166	  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.
167	
168	## Process observation
169	
170	The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
171	toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
172	close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
173	`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
174	initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
175	was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
176	delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
177	a more thorough process.
178	
179	The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
180	pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
181	correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
182	ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
183	emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
184	of the original user framing would balance the record.
185	
186	**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
187	action implied for the design itself).
188	
189	## Disposition table
190	
191	| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
192	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
193	| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
194	| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
195	| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
196	| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
197	| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
198	| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
199	| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
200	| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
201	| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |
202	
203	## TL;DR
204	
205	Items 1 and 3 are the bot's highest-conviction concerns —
206	the runtime `--por` semantics doc (now resolved on
207	framing but doc text still needs the rewrite) and the
208	`--init-from-recursive` surface doubling. The rest are
209	docs-only edits that don't block 0.62.0 starting on T7,
210	but each is cheaper to apply now (single design pass)
211	than after the next ten cycles have referenced the
212	current text.
213	
214	# References
215	
216	[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md
217	[2]: /notes/design-cli/copying.md
218	[3]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity.md

of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).

  stub from `0.61.0-4`.

  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality

  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling

  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability

**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a

is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp

**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
controls.

**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
that cycle starts.

### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out

**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
workspace with no remote for either side." The
bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
first-class or rejected at parse time.

**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
confused error later in the workflow.

**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
none` lands (Gap #5).

### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout

**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
ignored — match the broader chain rule."

The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
but it's not the obvious choice.

**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
same conversation in 0.62.0.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency

**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying

**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090

  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |

| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

Items 1 and 3 are the bot's highest-conviction concerns —
the runtime `--por` semantics doc (now resolved on

| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | `audit.md` |
| N4 | `validate-todo`/`fix-todo` topology-blind note | `audit.md` |
| Pr | Original user-framing acknowledgment | `chores-12.md` or `audit.md` |

After those, the **0.62.0 close-out** (bare version) remains.

Note: the `## In Progress` block in `todo.md` still reads `0.62.0-0 (current)` — it's stale relative to the four commits actually landed.

Want me to pick up with **0.62.0-5 (Concern #3)** and walk the remaining items, or focus somewhere specific?

message.content: continue with 0.62.0-5 (Concern #3)

content.content:
1	# copying (design stub)
2	
3	Forward-looking design capture from the 0.61.0 parity audit
4	cycle. No implementation yet — concrete work lands when a
5	follow-on cycle picks it up.
6	
7	Generalizes "copy these files into the new workspace at
8	init time" into one composable mechanism that subsumes
9	today's `--config <path>` (deprecated under the
10	`.vc-config.toml` write axis collapse —
11	see [[1]]), a hypothetical `--gitignore <path>`, and
12	today's `--use-template <CODE[,BOT]>`.
13	
14	The mechanism is **pure file copy** — no variable
15	substitution, no transformation. The bot deliberately
16	avoided "templating" / "seeding" as concept names; "seed"
17	implies growth and "template" implies parameterization,
18	neither of which this mechanism does. It just copies.
19	
20	## Goal
21	
22	One mechanism for "copy these files into the workspace at
23	init time" rather than per-file flags. The user supplies
24	one or more source paths; init copies them into the new
25	workspace during the copy phase, before commits.
26	
27	## Surface
28	
29	- `--init-from-code=<glob>` — copy files matched by
30	  `<glob>` into the code repo (non-recursive).
31	- `--init-from-bot=<glob>` — same, into the `.claude/`
32	  companion. Only meaningful under dual.
33	- `--init-from=<glob>` — shorthand for `--init-from-code`
34	  (since por only has code, and dual users typically copy
35	  into code more often than bot).
36	- `--init-from-recursive=<glob>` /
37	  `--init-from-code-recursive=<glob>` /
38	  `--init-from-bot-recursive=<glob>` — recursive variants.
39	
40	Each flag accepts a **shell glob** (expansion done by the
41	shell; `vc-x1` receives the expanded path list). Each may
42	be specified multiple times — the result is the additive
43	union of all sources. Order matters for collisions (see
44	below).
45	
46	## Behavior
47	
48	- **Copy semantics** — files only; preserves relative
49	  layout under the source root. The bot's working
50	  assumption is `cp -a` semantics (preserve symbolic
51	  links and executable bits); confirm at design time.
52	- **Collision: last writer wins, with a warning.** If two
53	  `--init-from*` flags resolve to the same destination
54	  file, the later one overwrites the earlier; `vc-x1`
55	  emits a warning naming both sources. The bot thinks
56	  warnings are right here — silent overwrite is a
57	  footgun; erroring would block valid layering use cases
58	  (a base directory + per-project tweaks).
59	- **Canned writes suppressed when copying is engaged.**
60	  Today `init` writes a canned `.vc-config.toml` and
61	  `.gitignore`. With any `--init-from*` present, **both
62	  canned writes are suppressed entirely**. The user opted
63	  in to full control of the copied content; init doesn't
64	  layer canned defaults underneath.
65	- **Dual post-copy check (deferred).** After copying
66	  completes for a dual workspace, if `.vc-config.toml`
67	  is missing or lacks `[workspace] other-repo`, init
68	  **warns** (does not error). The workspace looks dual
69	  (has `.claude/`) but acts por at runtime; downstream
70	  subcommands enforce the invariant. See
71	  [Deferred-validation](#deferred-validation).
72	
73	## Deferred-validation
74	
75	Init's `.vc-config.toml` check is a **warning**, not an
76	error, to support workflows where the user plans to
77	supply the config post-init (e.g. checking in the
78	workspace shell first, then populating config files in a
79	follow-up commit).
80	
81	The error happens at the **first downstream invocation**:
82	`default_scope` (or a new explicit check) detects the
83	broken-dual state — `.claude/` exists but `.vc-config.toml`
84	doesn't (or lacks `other-repo`) — and refuses to proceed
85	with a clear error pointing at the missing file.
86	
87	Today `default_scope` silently falls back to
88	`Scope([Code])` when `.vc-config.toml` is absent
89	(`src/common.rs:594`). That silent fallback is exactly
90	the "looks dual, acts por" surprise this design avoids;
91	fixing it is a prerequisite for the post-init copy
92	workflow.
93	
94	## Fixed filename
95	
96	`.vc-config.toml` stays the fixed name; overriding it is
97	deferred until a real use case surfaces (would ripple
98	through every subcommand's lookup code — `default_scope`,
99	`find_workspace_root_from`, `validate-desc`, `fix-desc`,
100	`push`, `clone`, …).
101	
102	## Subsumes
103	
104	- `--config <path>` — never really built; the
105	  `.vc-config.toml` write axis's collapse
106	  left the capability un-claimed. Equivalent under
107	  copying: `--init-from=<src>/.vc-config.toml`.
108	- `--gitignore <path>` — never built; equivalent under
109	  copying: `--init-from=<src>/.gitignore`.
110	- `--use-template <CODE[,BOT]>` — today's coarse
111	  whole-repo flag. Subsumed by recursive variants:
112	  - `--use-template code-tpl/` →
113	    `--init-from-code-recursive=code-tpl/*`.
114	  - `--use-template code-tpl/,bot-tpl/` → two flags:
115	    `--init-from-code-recursive=code-tpl/*` +
116	    `--init-from-bot-recursive=bot-tpl/*`.
117	
118	The bot thinks `--use-template` can be retired once
119	copying ships, but a back-compat shim that translates
120	old form → new form is cheap and worth keeping for one
121	release cycle.
122	
123	## Naming rationale
124	
125	The mechanism is named **copying** (not "templating," not
126	"seeding," not "scaffolding") because all three imply
127	something the mechanism doesn't do:
128	
129	- "Templating" implies parameterization / variable
130	  substitution. We deliberately don't do that.
131	- "Seeding" implies growth (a seed grows into a plant);
132	  the copied files don't grow — they sit where they
133	  landed.
134	- "Scaffolding" implies project-structure generation
135	  (Rails generators, Yeoman); the mechanism is just
136	  `cp -r`, no structure-aware logic.
137	
138	"Copying" is literal: source files go to destination
139	paths. The flag family `--init-from*` keeps the verb out
140	of the surface — the flag describes *what* (source-to-
141	destination), not *what kind of operation*. If a future
142	mechanism does layer substitution on top of copy, it's a
143	separate feature with its own name.
144	
145	## Open questions (resolve at design time)
146	
147	- **Variable substitution.** Should the copy step support
148	  `{{name}}` / `{{account}}` substitution? The bot
149	  thinks **no** — keep the mechanism pure copy. Users
150	  who need substitution can pre-process and pass the
151	  result. Adding substitution invites a substitution-
152	  language rabbit hole and breaks the "copying" framing
153	  we just settled on.
154	- **Symbolic links and executable bits.** Preserve, or
155	  flatten? Probably preserve — `cp -a` semantics.
156	  Worth confirming at design time.
157	- **Source-not-found behavior.** Hard error (typo
158	  protection) or soft skip (let globs return empty)?
159	  Probably hard error — a missing source is almost
160	  always user mistake. Empty glob expansion (the shell
161	  passing zero paths) is fine.
162	- **Interaction with `vc-x1 clone`.** Clone seeds from
163	  an existing remote, so copying overlays *on top of*
164	  the cloned content. Same flag set, same collision
165	  rules.
166	- **Mechanism beyond init?** A future
167	  `vc-x1 <subcommand> --copy-from=<glob>` to layer
168	  files into an existing workspace would be the same
169	  mechanism with a different host command. The bot
170	  thinks defer until a real use surfaces; `--init-from*`
171	  is the only surface today.
172	
173	## See also
174	
175	- `notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md > ## Feature axes > .vc-config.toml write — collapsed`
176	  — the collapse that motivates this design [[1]].
177	- `notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md > ## Feature axes > Copying`
178	  — today's `--use-template` and the negotiated
179	  decisions that this design subsumes.
180	
181	# References
182	
183	[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md#vc-configtoml-write--collapsed
184
